prs.title: test: avoid RegExp.prototype.test in crypto error assertion

labels.name: test
labels.description: Issues and PRs related to the tests.

labels.name: needs-ci
labels.description: PRs that need a full CI run.

prs.body:
This PR updates `test/parallel/test-crypto-no-algorithm.js` to align with the ongoing test cleanup and modernization work across the Node.js codebase.

As part of recent efforts, tests are being standardized to avoid using `RegExp.prototype.test()` directly inside assertions in favor of clearer, more explicit matching patterns. This change applies that same approach to a crypto test that validates OpenSSL error output.

## What was changed

Previously:

```js
assert(
  err.opensslErrorStack.some((msg) => expected.test(msg)),
  `did not find ${expected} in ${err.opensslErrorStack}`
);
```
Updated to: 

```js
assert(
  err.opensslErrorStack.some((msg) => msg.match(expected)),
  `did not find ${expected} in ${err.opensslErrorStack}`
);
``` 
## Why this change
- Aligns the test with modern Node.js assertion patterns used elsewhere in the test suite
- Improves readability by expressing intent as string matching rather than invoking `RegExp.prototype.test()`
- Keeps assertion style consistent with recent test modernizations

## Additional notes
- No functional or behavioral changes are intended
- Test logic, coverage, and failure messages remain unchanged
- All tests continue to pass

issues.title: Missing test for empty string in path.posix.normalize()
issues.body:
### Version

v21.6.2

### Platform

```text
Darwin MacBook-Pro.local 23.5.0 Darwin Kernel Version 23.5.0
```

### Subsystem

path

### What steps will reproduce the bug?

1. Open the file: lib/path.js.
2. Scroll to the implementation of path.posix.normalize().
3. Observe this branch:

     if (path.length === 0)
       return '.';

4. This defines expected behavior: path.posix.normalize('') === '.'

5. Now open the test file:
   test/parallel/test-path-normalize.js

6. Search for a test that covers:
   path.posix.normalize('') === '.'

7. There is no such test.

This means the empty-string branch in path.posix.normalize() is not covered by the test suite.


### How often does it reproduce? Is there a required condition?

Always. The test is always missing.
No special conditions are required.


### What is the expected behavior? Why is that the expected behavior?

Expected:
path.posix.normalize('') should return '.'

Reason:
The implementation explicitly returns '.' when the input path is an empty string:

if (path.length === 0)
  return '.';

This branch should be covered by a test to prevent regressions, since all other path.normalize edge cases are tested.


### What do you see instead?

There is no test covering path.posix.normalize('').

The test file test/parallel/test-path-normalize.js tests many cases, but does not include a test for the empty-string input.


### Additional information

I would like to submit a PR adding this missing test case.

This is not a functional bug but a test coverage enhancement.

issues.title: Add validation to reject SVGs containing `fill` attributes

labels.name: package
labels.description: Pull requests or issues that affect the NPM package.

labels.name: spam
labels.description: Issues and pull requests identified as spam.

issues.body:
### Kind of Issue

Bug

### This issue concerns the...

NPM package

### Package Version

11.0.0

### Other Software

Node.js v18
NPM v10
None


### Description

### Summary
Simple Icons enforces strict monochrome SVG rules, but the current validation does **not** catch SVGs that contain `fill` attributes. This allows invalid icons to pass CI.

---

### Steps to Reproduce
1. Add an SVG under `icons/` that contains a `fill="..."` attribute.
2. Run the validation/build scripts (`npm test`, `npm run build`, or CI).
3. The tests pass even though the SVG violates Simple Icons’ rules.

---

### Current Behavior
- Icons with `fill` attributes are **not rejected**.
- Validation/CI does **not** fail.
- This can lead to incorrect, non-monochrome icons being included in the package.

---

### Expected Behavior
The validation pipeline should:
1. Scan `icons/*.svg` for any `fill="..."` attributes.
2. Fail validation if any are found.
3. Display a clear error message such as:
   > “Icons must not contain `fill` attributes. Please remove all fill attributes from SVG files.”

---

### Why This Matters
- Ensures all icons follow the project's monochrome requirements.
- Prevents invalid icons from being accepted.
- Reduces manual review work.
- Improves package consistency and reliability.

---

### Proposed Fix
I would like to contribute a PR that:
1. Adds a new validation check (or test) to detect any `fill` attribute in SVGs.
2. Fails the build with a descriptive error.
3. Optionally corrects any existing icons that contain `fill`.

Please let me know if maintainers would accept this improvement.

issues.title: force-dynamic incorrectly forces entire route tree to become dynamic (create-component-tree.tsx bug)

labels.name: Performance
labels.description: Anything with regards to Next.js performance.

labels.name: Runtime
labels.description: Related to Node.js or Edge Runtime with Next.js.

labels.name: Parallel & Intercepting Routes
labels.description: Related to Parallel and/or Intercepting routes.

labels.name: Loading UI and Streaming
labels.description: Related to loading UI (loading.tsx) and streaming.

issues.body:
### Link to the code that reproduces this issue https://github.com/cruzz77/force-dynamic-bug ## **To Reproduce** 1. Create a new Next.js app using the reproduction template: ```bash npx create-next-app@latest --example reproduction-template force-dynamic-bug cd force-dynamic-bug ``` 2. Create the following file structure inside `app/`: ``` app/ ├── layout.tsx ├── page.tsx └── nested/ ├── layout.tsx └── page.tsx ``` 3. Add `export const dynamic = 'force-static'` to both layout files. **app/layout.tsx** ```tsx export const dynamic = 'force-static' export default function RootLayout({ children, }: { children: React.ReactNode }) { return ( {children} ) } ``` **app/nested/layout.tsx** ```tsx export const dynamic = 'force-static' export default function NestedLayout({ children, }: { children: React.ReactNode }) { return (

Nested Layout (should be static)

{children}
) } ``` 4. Add `export const dynamic = 'force-dynamic'` to the nested page. **app/nested/page.tsx** ```tsx export const dynamic = 'force-dynamic' export default function NestedPage() { return (

This page should be dynamic: {Date.now()}

) } ``` 5. Run the production build: ```bash npm run build ``` 6. Observe the behavior: - Parent layouts **with** `dynamic = "force-static"` are incorrectly forced to become **dynamic**. - Entire route branch becomes dynamic due to the nested page. - Static optimization for parent layouts is lost. ## **Current vs. Expected behavior** ### ❌ Current Behavior (Bug) When the nested page exports: ```ts export const dynamic = "force-dynamic" ``` Next.js incorrectly applies this dynamic setting to: - the parent layout (`app/layout.tsx`), - the nested layout (`app/nested/layout.tsx`), - and the entire route branch above it. This happens because `workStore.forceDynamic` is mutated globally during tree traversal inside: ``` packages/next/src/server/app-render/create-component-tree.tsx ``` As a result: - layouts explicitly marked `dynamic = "force-static"` are ignored - static generation is disabled for the entire branch - partial prerendering (PPR) is broken - unnecessary runtime rendering occurs - dynamic boundaries propagate upward when they should not Next.js’ own comment in the code acknowledges this is incorrect behavior: ```ts // @TODO this does not actually do what it seems like it would or should do. // ... the entire render becomes force-dynamic. // We should refactor this function so that we can correctly track which segments // need to be dynamic. ``` ### ✅ Expected Behavior Only the segment that explicitly declares: ```ts export const dynamic = "force-dynamic" ``` should be forced dynamic. The parent layouts **should remain static**, because they explicitly declare: ```ts export const dynamic = "force-static" ``` Therefore: - `/nested/page` → dynamic (correct) - `/nested/layout` → static (expected) - `/layout` → static (expected) Dynamic flags should **not propagate upward** to ancestors or sibling segments. ### Summary The current behavior breaks the documented App Router expectation that **dynamic/static settings are scoped per segment**. Instead, dynamic mode is applied to the entire tree, causing significant loss of static optimization. ### Provide environment information ```bash Please find the output of `next info` below: Operating System: Platform: darwin Arch: arm64 Version: Darwin Kernel Version 23.6.0 Binaries: Node: 20.17.0 npm: 10.8.2 Yarn: 1.22.22 pnpm: 9.10.0 Relevant Packages: next: 15.0.0-canary.148 eslint-config-next: 15.0.0-canary.148 react: 19.0.0-rc-3dfd5d9e-20240910 react-dom: 19.0.0-rc-3dfd5d9e-20240910 typescript: 5.6.2 Next.js Config: output: default ``` ### Which area(s) are affected? (Select all that apply) Parallel & Intercepting Routes, Partial Prerendering (PPR), Performance, Dynamic Routes, Runtime, Loading UI and Streaming ### Which stage(s) are affected? (Select all that apply) Vercel (Deployed), next start (local), next build (local), next dev (local) ## **Additional context** - The issue is reproducible locally and on Vercel deployments. - It occurs consistently across multiple canary releases. - The first canary where I detected the bug was: ``` 15.0.0-canary.148 ``` Rolling back to: ``` 15.0.0-canary.147 ``` still reproduces the issue since the bug originates from logic in `create-component-tree.tsx`, where `workStore.forceDynamic` is applied globally. - The behavior occurs regardless of hosting method (local, Vercel, Docker). - The issue is not browser-specific. - The bug stems from dynamic mode incorrectly propagating upward during tree traversal.

issues.body:

Create a new Next.js app using the reproduction template: ```bash npx create-next-app@latest --example reproduction-template force-dynamic-bug cd force-dynamic-bug ``` 2. Create the following file structure inside `app/`: ``` app/ ├── layout.tsx ├── page.tsx └── nested/ ├── layout.tsx └── page.tsx ``` 3. Add `export const dynamic = 'force-static'` to both layout files. **app/layout.tsx** ```tsx export const dynamic = 'force-static' export default function RootLayout({ children, }: { children: React.ReactNode }) { return ( {children} ) } ``` **app/nested/layout.tsx** ```tsx export const dynamic = 'force-static' export default function NestedLayout({ children, }: { children: React.ReactNode }) { return (

Nested Layout (should be static)

{children}
) } ``` 4. Add `export const dynamic = 'force-dynamic'` to the nested page. **app/nested/page.tsx** ```tsx export const dynamic = 'force-dynamic' export default function NestedPage() { return (

This page should be dynamic: {Date.now()}

) } ``` 5. Run the production build: ```bash npm run build ``` 6. Observe the behavior: - Parent layouts **with** `dynamic = "force-static"` are incorrectly forced to become **dynamic**. - Entire route branch becomes dynamic due to the nested page. - Static optimization for parent layouts is lost. ### Current vs. Expected behavior ## **Current vs. Expected behavior** ### ❌ Current Behavior (Bug) When the nested page exports: ```ts export const dynamic = "force-dynamic" ``` Next.js incorrectly applies this dynamic setting to: - the parent layout (`app/layout.tsx`), - the nested layout (`app/nested/layout.tsx`), - and the entire route branch above it. This happens because `workStore.forceDynamic` is mutated globally during tree traversal inside: ``` packages/next/src/server/app-render/create-component-tree.tsx ``` As a result: - layouts explicitly marked `dynamic = "force-static"` are ignored - static generation is disabled for the entire branch - partial prerendering (PPR) is broken - unnecessary runtime rendering occurs - dynamic boundaries propagate upward when they should not Next.js’ own comment in the code acknowledges this is incorrect behavior: ```ts // @TODO this does not actually do what it seems like it would or should do. // ... the entire render becomes force-dynamic. // We should refactor this function so that we can correctly track which segments // need to be dynamic. ``` ### ✅ Expected Behavior Only the segment that explicitly declares: ```ts export const dynamic = "force-dynamic" ``` should be forced dynamic. The parent layouts **should remain static**, because they explicitly declare: ```ts export const dynamic = "force-static" ``` Therefore: - `/nested/page` → dynamic (correct) - `/nested/layout` → static (expected) - `/layout` → static (expected) Dynamic flags should **not propagate upward** to ancestors or sibling segments. ### Summary The current behavior breaks the documented App Router expectation that **dynamic/static settings are scoped per segment**. Instead, dynamic mode is applied to the entire tree, causing significant loss of static optimization. ### Provide environment information ```bash Please find the output of `next info` below: Operating System: Platform: darwin Arch: arm64 Version: Darwin Kernel Version 23.6.0 Binaries: Node: 20.17.0 npm: 10.8.2 Yarn: 1.22.22 pnpm: 9.10.0 Relevant Packages: next: 15.0.0-canary.148 eslint-config-next: 15.0.0-canary.148 react: 19.0.0-rc-3dfd5d9e-20240910 react-dom: 19.0.0-rc-3dfd5d9e-20240910 typescript: 5.6.2 Next.js Config: output: default ``` ### Which area(s) are affected? (Select all that apply) Parallel & Intercepting Routes, Partial Prerendering (PPR), Dynamic Routes, Runtime, Loading UI and Streaming, Performance ### Which stage(s) are affected? (Select all that apply) next dev (local), next build (local), next start (local), Vercel (Deployed) ### Additional context ## **Additional context** - The issue is reproducible locally and on Vercel deployments. - It occurs consistently across multiple canary releases. - The first canary where I detected the bug was: ``` 15.0.0-canary.148 ``` Rolling back to: ``` 15.0.0-canary.147 ``` still reproduces the issue since the bug originates from logic in `create-component-tree.tsx`, where `workStore.forceDynamic` is applied globally. - The behavior occurs regardless of hosting method (local, Vercel, Docker). - The issue is not browser-specific. - The bug stems from dynamic mode incorrectly propagating upward during tree traversal.

issues.title: perf: plugin filter cache misses for hooks without filters
issues.body:
### Describe the bug

The `getCachedFilterForPlugin` function has a cache miss bug where it repeatedly recalculates `undefined` results for plugin hooks that don't have filters, causing unnecessary performance overhead.

**What I expect**: Once a plugin hook filter is calculated as `undefined`, subsequent calls should return the cached `undefined` result.

**What actually happens**: Every call for hooks without filters recalculates `undefined` instead of using cache.

**Reproduction Note**: This is an internal Vite code bug in the plugin system, so it cannot be reproduced via vite.new. The reproduction link points directly to the problematic code. The bug can be verified by examining the `getCachedFilterForPlugin` function logic.

I intend to submit a PR for this issue.

### Reproduction

https://github.com/vitejs/vite/blob/main/packages/vite/src/node/plugins/index.ts#L180-L220

### Steps to reproduce

This is an internal code logic bug that affects all Vite builds using plugins. The issue occurs during plugin execution:

1. **Examine the problematic code**:
   - Open: `packages/vite/src/node/plugins/index.ts`
   - Look at function: `getCachedFilterForPlugin` (lines ~180-220)

2. **Identify the bug**:
   - The cache check uses `hookName in filters` which returns `false` for `undefined` values
   - This causes repeated calculations instead of cache hits

3. **Observe the impact**:
   - Any plugin hook call without a filter triggers this bug
   - Each call recalculates the same `undefined` result
   - Performance degradation occurs during both `dev` and `build` commands

**Code verification**:
```typescript
// This demonstrates the broken caching behavior
const plugin = { name: 'test', transform() {} };
getCachedFilterForPlugin(plugin, 'load'); // calculates undefined
getCachedFilterForPlugin(plugin, 'load'); // should use cache but recalculates

### System Info

```shell
System Info not applicable - this is an internal code logic bug that affects all systems running Vite with plugins. The issue is in the core Vite codebase and doesn't depend on specific system configuration.
```

### Used Package Manager

npm

### Logs

Not applicable - this is a code logic/performance bug, not a runtime error that produces logs. The issue is in the caching mechanism of the plugin system and doesn't generate error messages or debug output.

### Validations

- [x] Follow our [Code of Conduct](https://github.com/vitejs/vite/blob/main/CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md)
- [x] Read the [Contributing Guidelines](https://github.com/vitejs/vite/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md).
- [x] Read the [docs](https://vite.dev/guide).
- [x] Check that there isn't [already an issue](https://github.com/vitejs/vite/issues) that reports the same bug to avoid creating a duplicate.
- [x] Make sure this is a Vite issue and not a framework-specific issue. For example, if it's a Vue SFC related bug, it should likely be reported to [vuejs/core](https://github.com/vuejs/core) instead.
- [x] Check that this is a concrete bug. For Q&A open a [GitHub Discussion](https://github.com/vitejs/vite/discussions) or join our [Discord Chat Server](https://chat.vite.dev/).
- [x] The provided reproduction is a [minimal reproducible example](https://stackoverflow.com/help/minimal-reproducible-example) of the bug.
